coredata.dc:title: Benzothiazole-based ionic liquids as environment-friendly and high-efficiency corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in HCl: Experimental and theoretical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Ling
Lv, Yanli
Dong, Yan
Liao, Haosen
Wu, Shiyong
Li, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Two benzothiazole ionic liquids – (E)-3-ethyl-2-(4-hydroxystyryl) benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium iodide ([EHSBT]I) and (E)-3-ethyl-2-(4-hydroxystyryl)benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium bromide ([EHSBT]Br) generate the outstanding protection when employed as green corrosion inhibitors for the steel against aggressive acid solution at different temperatures. The corrosion efficiency of [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br with the low dosage of 0.2 mM is 98.16 % and 89.68 %, respectively, at 303 K. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of [EHSBT]I decreases with the increased temperature and [EHSBT]Br is the opposite. Fortunately, both [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br exhibit the inhibition efficiency of greater than 95 % even when it achieves 333 K, which is ascribed to the dominant chemical adsorption. Likewise, the electrochemical investigation presents the increasement of the charge transfer resistance and interfacial electric double layer thickness due to their adsorption on the substrate, restraining the metal dissolution and hydrogen evolution. The findings of the surface structure and appearance using UV–vis, FT-IR, XPS, SEM-EDS, AFM suggest that the inhibitors can form the complex film ([EHSTB]I-Fe, [EHSTB]Br-Fe) to shield the metal from corrosive particulates via chemical bonding. The confirmation of the active sites and adsorption configuration through theoretical calculation such as DFT and MD, along with RDF, promotes a further comprehension of the corrosion mitigation mechanism, which coincides with the experimental conclusion that the preparation of a stable protective film realizes the superior inhibition performance against high temperature acid corrosion.

head.ce:title: Benzothiazole-based ionic liquids as environment-friendly and high-efficiency corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in HCl: Experimental and theoretical studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two benzothiazole ionic liquids – (E)-3-ethyl-2-(4-hydroxystyryl) benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium iodide ([EHSBT]I) and (E)-3-ethyl-2-(4-hydroxystyryl)benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium bromide ([EHSBT]Br) generate the outstanding protection when employed as green corrosion inhibitors for the steel against aggressive acid solution at different temperatures. The corrosion efficiency of [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br with the low dosage of 0.2 mM is 98.16 % and 89.68 %, respectively, at 303 K. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of [EHSBT]I decreases with the increased temperature and [EHSBT]Br is the opposite. Fortunately, both [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br exhibit the inhibition efficiency of greater than 95 % even when it achieves 333 K, which is ascribed to the dominant chemical adsorption. Likewise, the electrochemical investigation presents the increasement of the charge transfer resistance and interfacial electric double layer thickness due to their adsorption on the substrate, restraining the metal dissolution and hydrogen evolution. The findings of the surface structure and appearance using UV–vis, FT-IR, XPS, SEM-EDS, AFM suggest that the inhibitors can form the complex film ([EHSTB]I-Fe, [EHSTB]Br-Fe) to shield the metal from corrosive particulates via chemical bonding. The confirmation of the active sites and adsorption configuration through theoretical calculation such as DFT and MD, along with RDF, promotes a further comprehension of the corrosion mitigation mechanism, which coincides with the experimental conclusion that the preparation of a stable protective film realizes the superior inhibition performance against high temperature acid corrosion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, the rich and affordable steel has proved to be the fundamental material employed extensively in numerous fields in virtue of its beneficial physical, mechanical and technological properties [1,2]. The structural deterioration is ascribed to the interaction among metallic materials and air, water, carbon dioxide and the others, in the natural environment. The electrochemical corrosion may result in hydrogen embrittlement and increases of the fracture threat when the steel suffers from acid solution. The average annual global corrosion domination expenses achieve an egregious number - $ 2.5 trillion [3]. Due to the spontaneity of corrosion, considerable researchers and scholars in the corrosion field have taken corrosion mitigation as the target of the investigation against the tremendous financial losses and objectionable influence on the metal industry [3,4]. Those organic corrosion inhibitors containing electronegative heteroatoms (N, O, S, P) and unsaturated bonds, which are the most prevalent measure, are in favor of diminishing the injury to the metallic materials inside corrosive media [1,5,6]; and yet they violate the principle of green chemistry for the reason that they may cause environmental pollution and then affect personal safety due to the volatile, insoluble and non-biodegradability [1,3,4]. The environment-friendly corrosion inhibitors have attracted attention from a growing number of researchers with the enhanced awareness of environmental protection and security [6,7].

The ionic liquids (ILs) have been universally exploited as environment-friendly corrosion inhibitors to reinforce the resistance of metallic materials for the invasion of various aggressive media in engineering applications [6], which are superior to plant extracts, expired drugs, amino acids, biopolymers, surfactants on account of their excellent physical and chemical properties such as near non-volatility, non-flammability, low melting point, strong ionic conductivity, thermal and electrochemical stability [7]. In addition, the amphiphilic ILs disaggregate into the organic anions or cation parts which suggests that the effective compact packing is suppressed, facilitating the formation of micelles without delay to achieve the characteristic adsorption on the interface [8]. Nevertheless, the application is obstructed by the moderate corrosion depression effects. This deficiency can be successfully retrieved by alternating the molecular structure via simple synthesis [9]. Mobin et al. obtained the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 79.2 % after immersion of low carbon steel in 5 % HCl solution with 2 mM choline acetate (ChA) at 30℃. It could be found that ChA was attached to the surface through chemical adsorption depending on several spectrum measurements such as UV–vis, FTIR, Raman, XPS, SEM [5]. Moreover, the moderate inhibition efficiency is attributed to the slanted CH3COO− anion to Fe (110) slab. Tarun et al. investigated its corrosion inhibition resistance of 2-(1-(4-((furan-2-ylmethylene) amino)-5-thioxo-4,5-dihydro −1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl)-2-(1H-imidazol-2-yl) ethyl) isoindoline-1,3-dione (FTTI) for N80 steel in 15 % HCl solution at 30-60℃. The formation of Fe-FTTI hybrid film with respect to Langmuir type upon the steel hinders the charge transfer at the anode and cathode via dominantly adsorbs onto the anodic activation region. Furthermore, FTTI complex film containing indole and thiazole owns enough active sites to protect the steel perfectly in hot acid environment [10]. A nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) is more conducive to extending the life of carbon steel in acid solution when compared with nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), which is related to the enhancement of the coating steady drawn support from sulfurelement [6]. The influence of electronegative heteroatoms on the increase of the inhibition efficiency was sorted in sequence of S > N > O, as emphasized in literature [11]. A lamellar-structured IL@LDH-C6(m2im)2-I was fabricated by the layered double hydroxides (LDHs) coatings modified with ionic liquids, which enables the magnesium alloy to avoid erosion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution within a week [12]. The corrosion mitigation performances of [P4-VP]MI and [P4-VP]BBr were significantly better than others([P4-VP] MN3, [P4-VP]MSCN), when the steel was exposed to amyctic sulphuric acid. Because the anionic activity is strengthened by the continuous increase of π electrons and heteroatoms, which sends the cations deviating from the parallel configuration and creates a sizeable steric hindrance to produce unstable surface coverage [13].

Benzothiazole is generally accepted as environment-friendly building block, which has been widely used as an effective pharmacophore for the therapeutics of various diseases such as tumors, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, epilepsy, bacterial infection, inflammation and thrombosis [14–19]. Benzothiazole derivatives have extensive application prospects as corrosion inhibitor for metal in acidic media [20–26]. The corrosion inhibitors based on benzothiazole ionic liquids are rarely reported. Therefore, two benzothiazole ILs (E)-3-ethyl-2-(4-hydroxystyryl)benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium iodide ([EHSBT]I) and (E)-3-ethyl-2-(4-hydroxystyryl)benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium bromide ([EHSBT]Br) (Fig. 1 ) with different anions have been prepared and regarded as environment-friendly corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in the provocative hydrochloric acid. We adopted multiple measurements comprising static weight loss at various temperatures, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), atomic force microscope (AFM), adsorption isotherm models and theoretical calculations including density functional theory (DFT) calculations, molecular dynamics simulations(MD) to explicate the corrosion inhibition characteristics in terms of the structural perspective and provided a adsorption behaviour diagram on the inhibition mechanism. In this work, it could be verified that the benzothiazole ILs presented marvelous corrosion restriction when employed as corrosion inhibitors for the steel toward the assault of normothermia or hot acid solution, which might supply a fantastic management for guiding the fabrication of green corrosion inhibitors on the horizon.

The elemental composition of the utilized mild steel was shown as follows (wt%):S (0.004 %), P (0.012 %), Si (0.21 %), Al (0.028 %), Mn (0.44 %), C (0.18 %) and the balance of Fe. All samples were prepared by these treatments in sequence: burnishing with sandpapers in series on 180–1200 grades, degreasing with ethanol (AR grade), rinsing with deionized water and drying in cold air, and then preserving in the desiccator. The investigated corrosion environment was 1 M hydrochloric acid which was prepared by dilution of 37 % concentrated hydrochloric acid using deionized water. The inhibited solutions were formulated using 1 M HCl and inhibitors of various concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the corrosion inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structure of inhibitors ([EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br) is showed in Fig. 1. The synthetic routine and the preparation process are shown in Supplementary Information (Fig. S1, Fig. S2).

The static weight loss measurements were accustomed to estimate the corrosion resistance of the synthesized [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br ionic liquids [5,9,10]. The prepared specimens were submerged in 1 M HCl solution without and with the inhibitors at various concentrations levels for 6 h under the temperatures ranged from 303 K to 333 K with 10 K steps. Then the pieces were pulled out, removed corrosion products by wiping moderately, cleaned with water, dried out water in tepid atmosphere, and weighed precisely. The weight loss value noted was the average in the triplicate experiments for the reliability of the results. The corrosion rate (v) and inhibition efficiency (ηw) were calculated via the Eqs. (1, 2) [27,28,29],
（1）

where m0 and m are the mass in gram of the sample before and after 6 h immersion in test solution, A and t is the uncovered area in cm2 and immersion time in hours, respectively, v0 and v suggest corrosion rates in the investigated 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentrations of the inhibitors at diverse experimental temperatures.

The electrochemical measurements including EIS and PDP modules were actualized using Iviumstat electrochemical workstation via typical three-electrode cells in which the 1 cm2 exposed surface area of the specimen, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum sheet were severally considered as a working, reference and counter electrode. The Luggin capillary tip was positioned as near as possible to the exposed steel surface for reducing solution resistance appropriately [5]. An open circuit potential (OCP) test was carried out to the coupons which soaked into the corrosive environments for a period of time until an equilibrium-state potential of the working electrode’s surface was attained in advance of the electrochemical measurement. EIS was then executed within a 5 mV amplitude at the frequency range from 10 mHz to 100 kHz. The PDP curves of the bared steel were eventually acquired when the potential polarization range was in the plus-minus 200 mV with respect to OCP at a 2 mV/s sweep speed.

The UV–vis absorption spectroscopic technique was employed to observe the difference among the pure inhibitors and surface products of the steel dipped into the uninhibited media and inhibited media composing of an optimum inhibitor concentration (0.2 mM) for 6 h, which could affirm the development of the inhibitor/metal complex. Furthermore, the Perkin Elmer UV Winlab Data Processor and Viewer were used to analyze the data, while a Perkin Elmer UV–visible Lambda 900 spectrophotometer was applied to gather the spectra.

FTIR technique was applied to detect the change of the inhibitors before and after 6 h immersion of steel in test solution which validating nature and bonding type onto the steel surface in the range from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. The samples were prepared by KBr powdertabletting method, and after that the spectra of inhibitors and adsorbed Fe-IL complexes were collected using infrared spectrometer (Nicolet iS10).

The surface composition of steel samples (1 × 1 cm2) exposed to 1 M HCl in absent and present of the inhibitors for 6 h was investigated using XPS (Thermo fisher Nexsa G2). The high-resolution spectra of different elements such as C1s, O1s, N1s, S 2p, Fe 2p were obtained by deconvolution method so as to confirm intimate constituents of adsorbed layers on the substrate.

The scanning electron micrograph-energy dispersive spectrometry (SEM-EDS) with a 15 kV acceleration voltage was used on the steel surface before and after exposed to 1 M HCl solution in the absent and present with an appropriate amount (0.2 mM) of the proper inhibitors to gain profile information. Apart from the morphology about the surface absorbate, the ingredients of the surface layer could be acquired via SEM-EDS employing the instrument of Zeiss SIGMA 500 (Germany).
AFM (Demension Icon) was conducted to know the evolution in surface roughness of the coupons before and after in the above test solution.

DFT has been recommended for speculating the reactivity of the studied structure at present. In general, the structure optimization for [EHSBT]Br in molecular and cationic states that was achieved by hybrid DFT calculation to B3LYP extent with 6–311 + G (d, p) basis set in the aqueous phase using Gaussian 09 software [30]. However, lanl08 (d) basis set was used on iodine to procure the optimized structure of [EHSBT]I without a hitch. The relevant quantum chemical parameters were calculated and thoroughly elucidated in the text below. Besides, the charge characteristics contributed to the comprehension of inhibitor’s adsorption behavior, such as Mulliken charge distribution [28].

The optimized structure was acquired through Dmol3 module favored by a generalized gradient approximation (m-GGA) at DNP 4.4 basis set for electron computations under the solvation in Materials Studio software, Fukui index was subsequently calculated [31]. Moreover, the Forcite module was conditioned to attain a steady adsorption configuration for commentating the interaction between the investigated structure and the Fe(110) surface [29]. On the foundation of those previous reports, Fe(110) plane was considered to be the most uniform layer with the longest surface spacing among the cleaved planes [5,29]. The model was established as follows: originally, a Fe(110) crystal that was cleaved into four layers developed to a greater surface containing (12 × 12) supercell along the x and y orientation, and a 40 Å thickness vacuum layer was built on it to complete a periodic boundary box with length (29.79 Å), width (29.79 Å) and height (47.15 Å) in an effort to abstain from the arbitrary boundary effect on the authenticity of the interface. Subsequently, to put a constructed solvent model containing 150H2O, 5H3O+, 5Cl−, as well as single optimized molecule or cation ([EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br, [EHSBT]+) into the front box. Ultimately, the COMPASSII force field in the Forcite module was used to simulate the optimized system in which the iron atoms were immobilized, relevant period boundary parameters accompanied by simulation time of 500 ps with 1 fs time step at 298 K are in the NVT canonical ensemble. It is worth mentioning that all simulations should be performed under the most stable conformation to guarantee the reliability of the outcomes.

In addition, Forcite analysis could know the radial distribution function (RDF) which met demand for evaluating the interaction type based on the shortest distance of any atoms inside of the inhibitor from the substrate atoms. The ultimate radial function was deemed as the probability of detecting particle B within (r + dr) interval around particle A, which was determined as below equation (3) [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and concentration on the inhibition efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Weight loss experiments were employed to investigate the variation of corrosion inhibition efficiency (ηw) and corrosion rate (v) by temperature and concentration of the inhibitors, as shown in Fig. 2 . The two inhibitors caused a significant reduction in the corrosion rate of mild steel in hydrochloric acid, which suggested that the corrosion was efficiently restrained by covering the substrate surface. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of [EHSBT]I inhibitor with the same concentration was higher than that of [EHSBT]Br at equal temperature below 333 K. On the contrary, when the temperature was elevated to 333 K, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of [EHSBT]I was slightly lower than that of [EHSBT]Br. Furthermore, the values of corrosion inhibition efficiency remained above 90 % when the inhibitor with low concentration of 0.05 mM was added into the system at 333 K, which indicates that both inhibitors can significantly improve the corrosion resistance of the steel. These corrosion inhibition performances of [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br are the most excellent among the well cited articles on ILs which is presented in Table S1 [5,9,30,32–37]. Due to the identical cation in the inhibitor molecules, the difference of corrosion inhibition efficiency originates from the discrepancy in anions. The [EHSBT]I exhibits the superior corrosion inhibition performance compared with the other below 333 K for the reason that it has a greater size of I− which takes precedence over [EHSBT]Br to contact with the substrate and covers more areas. I− with strong reducibility is preferentially oxidized to iodine at high temperature, and then I3 − formed by the reaction of iodine with I− are dissolved in the aqueous phase accompanied the subdued electronic interaction [5], leading to the desorption of a small amount of inhibitors that have been adsorbed onto the surface and the decrement of the surface coverage region, and hence the corrosion inhibition efficiency decreases.

The interaction between [EHSBT]Br and metal surface enhanced with increasing temperature, demonstrating the emergence of chemical bonding force. In short, the above two inhibitors can successfully protect the mild steel in hydrochloric acid and still show the excellent corrosion resistance even at elevated temperature.

The stability of the protective film is determined by the effect of exposure time on the corrosion efficiency of the inhibitors, which enables to evaluate the service life of the inhibitors. The influence of immersion time was evaluated with the concentration of 0.1 mM at 303 K, as shown in Fig. 3 . It reflects the increase in corrosion inhibition efficiency with immersion time extending to 12 h. It can be inferred that the inhibitors preferred to release the active ingredient to cover more reactive sites of corrosion with the prolongation of time and thereby achieved the effective protection of the steel. The basically steady corrosion inhibition efficiency is observed for immersion time increasing from 12 to 48 h. This phenomenon suggests that the achievement of an adsorption–desorption equilibrium state, which might be attributed to the contamination of the aggressive electrolyte solution induced by the soluble metal chlorides [10,38,39]. Thereafter, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of [EHSBT]I decreases until the stable efficiency is reached at 96 h; the corrosion inhibition efficiency of [EHSBT] Br has been gradually decreasing with the prolongation of immersion time until it reaches a stable state at 120 h. The inhibition efficiency decreased to 97.65 % for [EHSBT]I and 93.34 % for [EHSBT]Br, respectively. The occurrence of the results is due to the desorption of a small amount of the inhibitors. Moreover, due to the existence of the same cation in [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br, the higher efficiency of [EHSBT]I in contrast to [EHSBT]Br is observed through the relatively complete surface coverage originated from the greater size iodide ions.

The key to the realization of corrosion inhibition in previous investigation is the physical and chemical adsorption between the metal surface activation position and the inhibitor, which can produce a stable and dense adsorption film to separate from the corrosion environment. In the experiment, different adsorption isotherm models were exploited to match the experimental data [10]. The adsorption between the inhibitor and the surface of mild steel was explored based upon Langmuir (Fig. 4 ), El-Awady, Frumkin, Tamkin and Freundlich isotherm models (Fig. S3). It can be seen from the c/θ-c at different temperatures in Fig. 4 that the fitted linear correlation coefficient (R2) is very close to 1. Therefore, the adsorption behavior of the two corrosion inhibitors on the metallic surface was the most appropriately described with Langmuir mono-layer adsorption isotherm. The adsorption isotherms and interrelate thermodynamic parameters were depicted as the relationship mentioned below [5,10,30,40]:

Where, c represents the concentration of the corrosion inhibitor (mM), R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J⋅K−1⋅mol−1), T is the absolute temperature (K), 55.5 is the value of molar concentration of water in solution, θ and Kads is the surface coverage calculated by weight loss study and adsorption equilibrium constant obtained through Langmuir isotherm, respectively.

As presented in Table 1 , the value of Kads (Fig. S4) is positively correlated with temperature which suggests that heating promotes the adsorption of inhibitors. The Kads values of [EHSBT]I are larger than that of [EHSBT]Br, and thus I− strengthens the electrostatic attraction and contributes to preparing a protective film consisting of the inhibitors on the surface. Moreover, the positive–negative sign is able to determine the direction of the reaction proceeding spontaneously [34,41,42]The positive values of enthalpy (ΔHads) are found in the fitting plots which imply that the adsorption of inhibitors upon the steel surface requires external supply of energy. Because of more active anion in [EHSBT]I, the adsorption process needs to absorb more energy and the adsorption difficulty increases in comparison with [EHSBT]Br. Additionally, the positive values of entropy (ΔSads) represent the entropy increase, which manifests that the confusion extent of the system increases. This phenomena may be assigned for the development of the system disorder in direction generated from the water which is squeezed into the aqueous solution by the inhibitor, as well as the energy absorbed tends to enhance the disorder of the system. The degree of entropy increase caused by [EHSBT]I is higher than that of [EHSBT]Br, which is probably related to the stability of adsorption products formed via inhibitors interacting with the metal atoms [5,10,40,43].

In a certain temperature range, thekineticparameters obtained through the alternation of the corrosion rate with the changing concentrations could analyze the corrosion inhibition mechanism. The Arrhenius diagram and transition state diagram of mild steel in 1 M hydrochloric acid containing different concentrations of inhibitors are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S5. Therefore, according to the fitting slope and intercept of the equation, the relevant activation parameters are calculated in Table 2 , including apparent activation energy (Ea), pre-exponential factor (A), apparent activation enthalpy (ΔHa) and apparent activation entropy (ΔSa). The relationship between temperature and corrosion rate was studied by Arrhenius and transition state equation with the following Eqs. ((8), (9)) [10,40,43].

Among them, the Arrhenius constant NA is 6.02 × 1023 mol−1, and the Planck constant h is 6.626 × 10−34 J ⋅ s.

The apparent activation energy Ea means the amount of energy changes from a normal molecule to an activated complex. The values of activation energy Ea increase, reflecting that the formation of the corrosion products is obstructed by decreasing the activated molecules. The number of molecules undergoing an effective collision is expressed as the pre-exponential factor A which shows a positive correlation with the number of active sites. In summary, Ea and A codetermine the reaction rate. Table 2 shows that both Ea and A increase with the addition of [EHSBT]I, while [EHSBT]Br is the opposite. It reveals that the addition of [EHSBT]I quickens the collision frequency. However, the energy barrier that needs to be overcome to generate activated molecules is greatly elevated, which increases the difficulty of corrosion ions activation and decreases the corrosion rate observably. The supplement of Br− promotes the preparation of activated products, meanwhile, the substrate surface is concealed with the [EHSBT]Br adsorption film which lessens themagnitude of corrosive ions that induce the effective collision. △Ha and △Sa represent the activation enthalpy and the activation entropy, respectively. When △Ha > 0, the dissolution of metal by hydrochloric acid is a slow endothermic process and high temperature provides more energy to accelerate the deterioration of metal. The change trend of Ea and ΔHa is accordant with the addition of corrosion inhibitor, which is consistent with ΔHa = Ea - RT. When △Sa < 0, it implies that the slow process of metal atoms forming activated complexes is the rate-determining step instead of the diffusion and dissociation process. [EHSBT]I has the trend of entropy increase by contrast with the blank, because it hinders the emergence of activated complexes, and triggers the increase of system confusion degree through the adsorption of [EHSBT]I, which indicates that the corrosion is more difficult [40]. Besides, the activation process of corrosive ions is boosted by the adding [EHSBT]Br, and a more stable adsorption structure to generate on the surface augments the orderliness of the system accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The open circuit potential (OCP) versus time
ce:section.ce:para: The variation of OCP with time for the steel immersion in the test solution at different temperatures are displayed in Fig. S6. The findings of the stable potential during a period of 3600 s indicate that the system reaches an electrochemical steady-state, which is the prerequisite to attain reliable electrochemistry results. The variation of OCP is lower than 85 mV in inhibited surrounding in comparison to blank 1 M HCl at all temperatures, which reflects the safety of the cathodic and anodic region of corrosion for the adsorption of the inhibitors. Therefore, the inhibitors act as mixed type inhibitors for the steel to inhibit the corrosion of HCl [10,44–46].

The polarization curves for mild steel immersed in 1 M hydrochloric acid solution without inhibitor and with corresponding concentration inhibitors at various temperatures were exhibited in Fig. 6 . The polarization parameters such as corrosion current density (icorr), corrosion potential (Ecorr), cathode and anode slope (βc, βa) in Table 3 were obtained by curve extrapolation method. The values of inhibition efficiency (ηi) were also figured outrelying on Eq. (10).
(10)
η i
=
i 0
- i
/
i 0
× 100 %

As depicted in Fig. 6, there is no quite different from the profile of polarization curves containing the two inhibitors, which signifies that the difference of anions has no significant effect on the corrosion inhibition mechanism. Obviously, the corrosion current density for steel adding [EHSBT]I or [EHSBT]Br is apparently reduced by comparison with the uninhibited control group. The current density values of [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br decrease/increase with the increase of inhibitor concentration, respectively. It can be inferred that low-dosage [EHSBT]I has stronger inhibition performance, whereas relatively high concentration of [EHSBT]Br manages to cover more active sites on the surface of steel, and the corrosion resistance is more excellent. Furthermore, the deviations of Ecorr are less than 85 mV which indicates that both [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br are regarded as mixed-type inhibitors. The oxidation of the metal atoms at the anode is hindered by the inhibitors, while the release of hydrogen at the cathode can also be suppressed. In contrast to the system without corrosion inhibitors, the corrosion potential (Ecorr) value slightly moves to the negative direction after adding [EHSBT]I, which elucidates that cathodic reduction reaction is mainly restricted by [EHSBT]I adsorption on the surface cathode region, and thereby the purpose of preventing corrosion is excellently achieved. The alteration in concentration and temperature affects the direction and degree of corrosion potential shift. Compared with the blank solution, the corrosion potential of [EHSBT]Br is slightly positive at 303 K and 323 K, while the corrosion potential at 333 K is more negative, which can be conjectured that anode inhibition is dominant below 333 K. The part of the inhibitors physically adsorbed on the surface of the anode area will be desorbed when the temperature reaches 333 K, after that the inhibitor prefers to accept the electrons transferred from the anode area and adsorbs in the cathode active area, occupying the active site of hydrogen ion reduction reaction, and hence the cathode inhibition effect is even more pronounced.

Comparedwiththeblankgroup, Tafel slopes (anodic (βa) and cathodic (βc))，do nothavetoogreatchanger, revealing that the adsorption film formed by the inhibitor effectively prevented metal oxidation and hydrogen evolution which relied on hindering the reaction of corrosion active sites rather than changing the corrosion mechanism. There is not much difference between βa and βc which further illustrate that mild steel corrosion is controlled in both directions. The values of βc decrease when adding inhibitors, which indicates which indicates that the double layer thickening of the metal-solution interface hinders the reduction process due to the adsorption of the inhibitors [10,47–49]. The values of βa decrease as the temperature increases in Table 3 which implies that the inhibitors may adsorb to the mild steel surface to form a denser protective film at high temperatures. In addition, probably due to the suppression of the anodic and cathodic electrochemistry by the inhibitor, the Tafel slopes exhibit irregular changes in the presence of the inhibitors compared to the blank HCl [43].

In consequence, the polarization curves shift to the direction of the polarization current density reduction when the solution is supplemented with either [EHSBT]I or [EHSBT]Br, inferring that the metal dissolution is hindered by the inhibitor protective film to mitigate corrosion effectively [10,30,35,50,51].

The corrosion resistance of metallic substrate in HCl with participation and absence of inhibitors was obtained by EIS to clarify the corrosion inhibition mechanism. The Nyquist plots exhibit a single compressed capacitance semicircle in Fig. 7
. These semicircles reveal the domination role of charge transfer in corrosion, and only one time constant is definited by the single circle. The imperfect semicircle indicates the unideal capacitance behavior over the substrate, which is ascribed to the surface heterogeneity in the stage of adsorption [40]. The larger diameter of the curves for steel in inhibited electrolyte than that in the blank solution is observed from the Nyquist diagram, indicating that the increased charge transfer resistance is caused by the generation of a protective film on the steel surface. Besides, the similar semicircular profile means that the corrosion process is mitigated by the addition of inhibitors rather than change in mechanism. The decrease of capacitive loop size at elevated temperatures is due to the desorption of the inhibitors at the same concentration. The capacitive loop size is observed to decrease with the increasing [EHSBT]I concentration at 303 K and 333 K while a positive correlation occurs at 323 K, suggesting that the compactness of protective film is probably altered due to the change of the spatial structure of [EHSBT]I affected by temperature. The capacitive loop size increases with [EHSBT]Br concentration at all temperatures because it may facilitate the formation of a barrier via the more [EHSBT]Br adsorbed on the steel surface [43]. The charge transfer resistance is higher for [EHSBT]I below 333 K, but [EHSBT]Br shows the better advantage than [EHSBT]I at 333 K. This implies that the larger size of [EHSBT]I, which is benefit to form a barrier covering the more areas, is adverse to the stability of the protective film at elevated temperatures in contrast to [EHSBT]Br [10,40].

The Bode diagram is shown in Fig. 9 which contains amplitude-freq and phase-freq curves. The impedance modulus for mild steel after exposure to the inhibited system increase compared to the blank, reflecting a stronger inhibitory capacitance in association to the formation of the compact film on the substrate. The alteration trend of the impedance modulus is consistent with the impedance in the Nyquist plots. Moreover, Bode phase curves can be used to interpret the surface roughness, and the more negative phase corresponds to the more flattened surface and less active sites of acid corrosion. The phase angle does not change obviously with inhibitors dosage at all temperatures, indicating that a uniform film is established on the substrate through the adsorption of the inhibitors. The increase in the frequency range supports the slow state for the attachment of the inhibitors. The appearance of the phase angle less than 90° indicates a deviation from the ideal electrochemical behavior, which is owing to the heterogeneity of metal substrate. The observation of the single peak reveals the one relaxation phenomenon which is concerning to the presence of electrical double layer with one time constant [43]. In general, the phase angle with [EHSBT]Br is more negative than that with [EHSBT]I, specifying that [EHSBT]Br is more conductive to the creation of a uniform and stable thin film.

Due to the roughness of the substrate surface, the R(QR) equivalent circuit model (Fig. 8) was used to obtain the relevant parameters of electrochemistry, and the results of charge transfer resistance (Rct), constant phase element (CPE), solution resistance (Rs), CPE related parameters and double layer capacitance (Cdl) are listed in Table 4 . The calculation was executed as the following Eqs. (11),12) [5]:

The observation in Table 4 shows that the dispersion index n approaches 1, which implies that the surface is capacitive. The values of n are smaller for steel in the solution with [EHSBT]I than those with [EHSBT]Br, which may be related to the interaction strength of the agent on the surface and interface properties. The charge transfer resistance increases significantly and the Cdl on the steel surface decreases when entering [EHSBT]I or [EHSBT]Br. The addition of corrosion inhibitors can exclude water molecules and replace them to adsorb on the surface, the dielectric constant decreases, and the thickness of the fabricated film increases, giving rise to a decrease in Cdl. Because the effective adsorption of inhibitors on the surface is beneficial to increase the charge transfer resistance and reduce the double layer capacitance, the corrosion rate is significantly decreased. The admittance Y0 can be served to describe the fluency of the load charge passing through the conductor, which is the inverse of the impedance. It can be seen that the admittance Y0 is decreased obviously with the addition of these two agents, which proposes that the charge transfer is blocked and the structural properties are changed [5,10]. The chi-square values express that the reasonable results of the fitting circuit have great reproducibility and the obtained inhibition efficiencies are consistent with those using weight loss.

The UV–vis spectrum analysis was conducted to verify the advancement of the iron/inhibitor complex. The UV–vis spectra depict pure inhibitor, product of steel surface before and after immersed in the studied 1 M HCl solution acid with and without inhibitors in Fig. 10 (a, b). [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br severally display a narrow adsorption band at 227–243 nm and 228–242 nm, which is primarily credited to the π-π* transition of the aromatic ring derived from the inhibitor. Due to sulfur, large conjugated system and anions, the wavelengths of electronic transition are red-shifted to the visible region over 400 nm [52,53]. A very weak absorption peak at 334 nm exhibits the spectrum of steel exposed to the uninhibited solution, which can be inferred to generate a complex through the interaction between chloride ions and hydrated ferrous ions. Compared with pure inhibitors, the absorption band is blue-shifted to 224–229 nm after interaction with iron, and the absorption peaks at 430 nm and 560 nm disappear from the visible region, along with the absorbance decreased dramatically. The above findings approve the inhibitors succeed in forming a complex structure via chemical bonding with ferrous ions. Compared with the steel in the uninhibited solution, the disappearance of the peak at 334 nm and the appearance of the peak at 229 nm suggest that the inhibitors are remarkably adsorbed onto the surface for hindering the chloride ions further adhere to ferrous ions [5,54]. As a conclusion, both inhibitors are supposed to create a complex film at the interface through chemical adsorption accompanying physical adsorption to establish secure protection measures.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) could validate the interaction between the inhibitor and iron by altering the compound construction, which facilitates the formation of a compacted film speculated by electrochemical tests. Fig. 11 shows the FT-IR spectra of pure inhibitor ([EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br), and the surface products on the specimen after exposure to 1 M hydrochloric acid in the presence and absence of the inhibitors. For pure [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br, the peaks located at 3450, 1260, and 1070 cm−1 are assigned for the stretching vibration of O–H connected to the benzene, C-O in the phenol, C-N+ in the thiazole, respectively [5,11]. The peaks of C = C stretching, C = N stretching, unsaturated benzene skeletal vibration, and the C–H bending vibration from the thiazole in [EHSBT]I are 1650, 1580, 1460, 1120 cm−1, while those in [EHSBT]Br emerge at 1590, 1520, 1450, 1130 cm−1 [11,53,55]. The pure [EHSBT]Br peak at 3120 cm−1 is ascribed to C = C–H stretching shown in Fig. 11 (b). Interestingly, those peaks in the range of 1390–1330 cm−1 disappear after interacting with the sample compared to the pure inhibitors. The difference is that a new peak appears at 3130 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1, corresponding to [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br adsorbed on the substrate, respectively [5,10,11,54]. In addition, the peaks disappear at 747, 1520 cm−1, and the same peaks are blue-shifted with 10 cm−1. All the above findings can sustain that the molecular structure change is credited to the chemisorption by bonding between inhibitors and iron atoms. Accordingly, [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br tend to form a complex film, increasing the eroded difficulty of aggressive acid solution [5,10,11,53–56].

XPS was executed to probe the elemental composition of the adsorptionfilm on the substrate surface. The high resolution XPS spectra of C, O, N, Fe and S on the substrate after immersion in 1 M HCl of adding inhibitors were displayed in Fig. 12 and Fig. 13 .

The appearance of three peaks in C1s high resolution deconvoluted spectra for the studied specimen represents the existence of various functional groups on the substrate surface. The peak at 284.78 eV and 284.58 eV reflects the presence of C–C, C = C and C–H bonds for [EHSBT]I (Fig. 12 (a)) and [EHSBT]Br (Fig. 13 (a)), respectively [10,57]. The peak at 286.48 eV in [EHSBT]I and 286.18 eV in [EHSBT]Br is due to the functionalities such as C- S, C-N, C-N+, C-O. The peak at 287.98 eV for [EHSBT]I and 288.28 eV for [EHSBT]Br verifies the presence of C = N on the protected surface [58].

Similarly, three peaks are also observed from the O1s spectra in Fig. 12(b) and Fig. 13(b), corresponding to the corrosion products such as FeO/Fe2O3 (529.78 eV for [EHSBT]I and 529.48 eV for [EHSBT]Br), OH− of the FeOOH (531.28 eV for [EHSBT]I and 530.98 eV for [EHSBT]Br) and C-O (532.48 eV for [EHSBT]I and 532.28 eV for [EHSBT]Br) [3,59], respectively.

The emergence of nitrogen signals in N1s spectra confirms theformationof protection layer by the adsorption of the inhibitors. Each peak was deconvoluted into two peaks where the peak at 399.98 eV and 399.48 eV which is ascribed to pronated nitrogen (C-/=N+) and C = N over inhibitor adsorbed surface; while the peak at 401.08 eV and 400.78 eV is due to the presence of N-Fe bond [11,57,60].

The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 12 (e)) is found as doublet for [EHSBT]I where 2p3/2 is deconvoluted into two peaks at 706.48, 711.01 eVrelating to Fe0 and FeOOH and the peak of 2p1/2 at 724.6 eV is in accordance with Fe2O3 / FeO. The peaks at 719.58 eV and 733.68 eV are assigned to Fe3+ of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively [10,61,62]. The 2p3/2 is deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 13(e)) demonstrating the presence of metallic Fe (706.28 eV), FeOOH / Fe-S (709.98 eV) and Fe3+ (712.78 eV) in case of [EHSBT]Br. The slight corrosion of steel surface is verified by the occurrence of metallic Fe, indicating that both [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br show the superior corrosion inhibited capability [11,63]. The peaks of S 2p3/2 are described by 162.08 eV, 162.68 eV for [EHSBT]I(and 163.48 eV, 163.78 eV for [EHSBT]Br in Fig. 12 (d) and Fig. 13 (d), which is due to the formation of Fe-S [11,64]. The peaks of S 2p1/2 are deconvoluted into 163.18 eV, 163.98 eV for [EHSBT]I and 164.78 eV, 164.98 eV for [EHSBT]Br, which are corresponding to C = S/C-S [11,65].
According to the results obtained through XPS analysis, a complex protective film of corrosion inhibitors forms on the substrate, which effectively reduce corrosion from HCl solutions.

SEM-EDS was exploited to observe the difference in surface morphology changes of mild steel exposed to the inhibitor present and absent hydrochloric acid. Fig. 14 (a) shows that the sample surface before hydrochloric acid erosion is relatively uniform and smooth, with some visible scratches. The steel surface immersed in 1 M hydrochloric acid without inhibitors is exceptionally rough and appears with numerous deep cavities simultaneously, as shown in Fig. 14 (b). The surface topography of the steel in the solution containing [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br are displayed in Fig. 14 (c) and Fig. 14 (d) individually. It is delightful to view that their scratches become relatively less evident, and the surface of both are not damaged by acid. The innovative phenomenon is that the steel surface is covered by considerably uniform ellipse-like substances, which differs from the corroded surface. Moreover, the circle unit of [EHSBT]I covering the surface is significantly more prominent compared with [EHSBT]Br due to the difference in anion sizes. According to what we have discovered above, it may conclude that the inhibitor adsorbed at the activated sites forms a thin coating via self-assembly approach that prevents surface contacting with invasive media. What’s more, Fig. 14 (e-h) shows the elemental composition for the surface corresponding to Fig. 14 (a-d) by energy spectrum analysis. In contrast to the freshly burnished steel specimen (Fig. 14 (e)), the weight percentage of iron for the coarse surface without inhibitor decreases, while carbon and oxygen increases. However, oxygen increases less, as illustrated in Fig. 14 (f). Apparently, Fig. 14 (g) and Fig. 14 (h) reflect the elemental composition of the piece that suffers from the inhibited system with [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br, respectively. As expected, the peak of sulfur arises, and nitrogen and oxygen weight percentage increase significantly as well, manifesting that the inhibitor is effectively adsorbed onto the surface to avoid deterioration. The weight percentage of iron with [EHSBT]Br is remarkably higher than [EHSBT]I, which advocates that the greater areas are concealed by larger iodide ions, corresponding to the larger dimension in the topography. Additionally, the magnitude of carbon and oxygen peaks augments and iron peak attenuates for the steel encountering the solution supplemented with the inhibitor, which prefers that both inhibitors adsorb on the surface to enhance corrosion resistance against acid.

The AFM profiles offer a detailed understanding of the mild steel substrate surface morphology immersed into 1 M HCl solution with the supplement of the inhibitors ([EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br). The average roughness (Ra) is associated with surface evenness. The observation of Fig. 15 (a) illustrates that the freshly polished surface is relatively smooth with some scratches and Ra of 90 nm. The AFM diagram of the surface in the uninhibited acid solution (Fig. 15 (b)) exhibits an entirely rough plane (Ra = 271 nm) with the majority of irregular pits originating from the struggle against aggressive hydrochloric acid. The surface exposed to the solution containing the inhibitors was uniform, and the values of Ra are reduced from 271 nm to 72 nm and 75 nm for [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br, respectively. The Ra has remarkably decreased, indicating that a more flat framework is generated to avoid acid corrosion due to the addition of the two inhibitors. Consequently, the adsorption of the inhibitors [EHSBT]I / [EHSBT]Br upon the substrate surface prevents the aggression acid, which can be demonstrated simultaneously.

Nowadays, theoretical calculations derived from density functional theory (DFT) have been ubiquitously availed to estimate the reactivity of particles. EHOMO is positively correlated with electron-donating ability, and ELUMO is negatively correlated with electron-receiving ability on the ground that the frontier molecular orbital theory [30,66,67]. The energy gap between ELUMO and EHOMO can qualitatively determine the reactivity of the moleculeswithsimilarstructures. The smaller the energy difference represents a stronger sensitivity to bonding [10,40]. It can be seen from Fig. 16 that [EHSBT]I with the lowest energy gap has the highest reactivity on account of the active iodide ion. Although the iodide ions approach to the surface entirely relying on electrostatic attraction as opposed to bonding by transferring electrons, the electron distribution of the HOMO-3 (the third orbital below the HOMO) with similar energy is concentrated on the cation part, indicating that the active areas of bonding with the metal surface are still determined by its cation [58]. However, the bromide ion in [EHSBT]Br expands the nucleophilic active region to almost the whole molecule in coordination with the cation. The stability of the adsorption membrane framework composed of [EHSBT]I is relatively abated in contrast to that of [EHSBT]Br for the reason that I− reduces the magnitude of active affinity sites. Furthermore, the electron-accepting active region of the LUMO orbitals before and after the dissociation of the inhibitors is focused on the cationic skeleton in containing the C = C double bond, heteroatoms (N, S, O), benzene rings in Fig. 16, and the ELUMO of [EHSBT]I or [EHSBT]Br is more than [EHSBT]+, which proposes that the anion slightly weakens the electrophilic character [35,40,68]. According to the depiction of the electrostatic potential (ESP) Fig. 16, there is negative and positive electricity concentrated on the anions and cations, respectively, revealing that the nucleophilic and electrophilic active sites are virtually throughout the entire molecule for the sake of electron transfer with the surface.

Based on hard and soft acid-base theory, iron was treated as soft acid that was sensitive to combine with the soft base to prepare a stable complex. The global hardness (η) of the [EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br and [EHSBT]+ is 1.466 eV, 1.604 eV and 1.603 eV, respectively, and the global softness (s) is in inverse proportionto global hardness. The data show that [EHSBT]I bonding with the metal surface is stronger to establish more effective protection for steel [13,69]. χ and ω can also reflect the reactivity, in which χ represents the ability to bind electrons, and ω refers to the combination ability toward electrophilic reagent. The appearance of a small χ and a large ω indicates the superior reactivity of the inhibitors [31,70]. These parameters were achieved by the Eqs. (15–24). [28–30,51,71].

Where φFe(110) is 4.82 eV, ηFe is zero [72].

The data in Table 5 leads us to conclude that [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br have higher reactivity than the dissociated [EHSBT]+ due to introducing a more active anion. △N can be used to confirm the direction and degree of electron transfer. On the premise of △N < 3.6, the inhibitor transfers electrons to the metal surface on condition that it is positive. Conversely, the electron transfer is in the opposite orientation [14,29]. The △N of [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br are 0.0706 and 0.03, respectively, and yet the △N of [EHSBT]+ is −0.0019, indicating that molecules are inclined to transfer electrons to the d-orbital of the iron and attach onto the substrate through the coordination bond. The non-bonding orbital of [EHSBT]+ has no trouble receiving the electrons released from the surface iron atoms, which facilitates the formation of a feedback bond. Because anions such as I− and Br− have a large volume, the electronic motion is most possibly confined around it so that the electron-donating center on the HOMO orbital is focused on it [56]. The dissociated [EHSBT]+ abandons the intervention of I− on the charge distribution, resulting in a weakened electron bonding ability on the HOMO orbital, which promotes the formation of feedback bonds instead [73,74]. △Eb-d is the feedback energy that signifies the strength of a feedback bond, and it can be found that △Eb-d of [EHSBT]+ is lower than molecules which supports that the retro donation is the most intensive. Hence [EHSBT]+ is effortless to accept electrons and then is adsorbed at the interface [75]. Besides, μ is closely related to polarity. There are two controversies about the relationship between corrosion inhibition performance and dipole moment. One is that small dipole moment is beneficial to the accumulation of inhibitors to build up better protection for the surface [76,77], and the other opposite view supports that large dipole moment furnishes more convenience to adsorption through strong dipole–dipole interaction [78]. The dipole moments in this work are observed to follow a tendency, where the values of dipole moment for [EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br and [EHSBT]+ are 21.36 D, 19.42D and 4.92 D in aqueous phase, respectively, which supports the latter viewpoint. Their values are larger than that of H2O (1.85D), clearly stating that the inhibitors are able to adsorb onto the substrate in preference to water [79]. However, the adsorption of [EHSBT]I depends on I−, and the cations of the corrosion inhibitor bind to the surface by giving and receiving electrons as I− is dissociated at high temperature, increasing active sites and assembling a smooth and compact film to block the invasion of corrosion ions. In contrast, the presence of Br− has little effect on the reactivity but increases the dipole moment and sustains the adsorption of cations onto the surface [80]. Since the reactive sites are nearly throughout the whole cation, a high-temperature resistant adsorption structure is generated. The interaction energy △Ψ of [EHSBT]I is higher than that of [EHSBT]Br, implying that [EHSBT]I has a stronger interaction with the substrate. Yet high temperature promotes anion to escape, then abating the interaction between [EHSBT]+ and the substrate.

The Mulliken charge distribution is commonly devoted to predicting the activity of the reaction zone, and negative and positive charge corresponds to nucleophilicity and electrophilicity, respectively [75]. Fig. 17 describes the topical charge distribution of [EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br and [EHSBT]+. The electrophilic active sites of [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br are localized at the negatively charged S, O, C = C, while N and C on the benzene ring with para-position of hydroxyl are more susceptible to be invaded by nucleophile, and the benzene ring structure is provided with amphiphilicity [74]. I− enhances the electron-donating ability of S and the electron-accepting ability of the benzene ring connected to thiazole, and the electron-accepting ability of N is slightly weakened. Therefore, the bonding effect with surface metal atoms is improved. In addition, Br− can also heighten the electron density around double bond (C = C) and the benzene ring linked to the hydroxyl group, along with the increased electron-donating activity of the adsorption areas, realizing that the stability of the surface adsorption construction is strengthened by the donation and acceptance of electrons cycle [29,80].

Likewise, Fukui index analysis can reflect atoms which contribute to combining with the metal surface and their bonding features. The Fukui function (ƒ− and ƒ+) is obtained through the mentioned Eqs. below [31].

Where (N), (N + 1) and (N − 1) represents the charge of the neutral, the anion with obtaining an electron and the cation derived from the departure of an electron, respectively. High and severally reveals the strong reactivity to electrophile and nucleophile attacks [28,31,52,81,82]. It stands to reason that heteroatoms, double bonds and benzene rings provide abundant active sites for adsorption in Fig. S7, which was consistent with the conclusion of Mulliken charge analysis [72,77,83]. Nevertheless, anions are no more than to extenuate their electron-donating ability, testifying that the speculation for HOMO orbital based on DFT is appropriate as well.

Quite apart from the adsorption structure of inhibitors on the surface, molecular simulation further elucidates the inhibition mechanism by considering the interaction strength. As shown in Fig. 18 , all of [EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br and [EHSBT]+ are adsorbed parallel to the interstitial sites of Fe on the surface, which impels water and corrosive ions alienated from the surface and accomplishes effective adsorption count on the donor–acceptor electron circulation between the active cation and the surface iron. The following Eqs. (27), 28) are replicated to calculate the adsorption and binding energies for the investigated configuration [30,52,84,85]:

In where, Etotal, Esol+sur represent the total energy of the system with and without inhibitor molecule, respectively. Einh represents the energy of the inhibitor molecule. The Eint values of [EHSBT]I, [EHSBT]Br and [EHSBT]+ were −277.23 kJ/mol, −274.68 kJ/mol and −255.13 kJ/mol, respectively. The negative values of the Eint for them on Fe (110) surface indicate the strong combination between the inhibitor and metal. The more negative values of Eint over Fe (110) plane for [EHSBT]I are corresponding to the stronger anti-corrosion performance compared with [EHSBT]Br, the better binding is reflected by their cation interacting with the surface which emphasizes that the cation occupies the predominant position in the adsorption, and the anion just builds a bridge to connect with the surface. Accordingly, the excellent inhibition capability of them at elevated temperatures validates the formation of a stable film which is also inferred by the experimental results.

Besides that, the radial distribution function can acquire the mode and essence of the interaction between the inhibitor and metal atoms of superficies on steel. In general, the distance corresponding to the first peak is 1–3.5 Å, which indicates that the chemical bond produces between different atoms [22,43,60]. Fig. 19 illustrates the RDF analysis of entire inhibitor on Fe (110) plane, the conjugation of the cations with iron relies heavily on heteroatoms, π electrons (C = C), and benzene rings as the anions are close to the surface with the assistance of electrostatic attraction. The above findings reflect that the inhibitor coordinate and back-donate with iron by electron transfer to assemble a complex film through these abundant active sites, thereby efficiently protecting the iron from acid attack.

On the metal surface, inhibitors get adsorbed through the proposed mechanism, as depicted in Fig. 20 . The surface of the steel is immersed in hydrochloric acid solution with positive charge because of its electron deficiency [5], and then it gradually adsorbs corrosive chloride ions to release electrons until the metal is dissolved at the anode. The process is shown in the following equations (1–4) [11]:
(1) Fe + n H2O → [Fe (H2O) n] ads
(2) [Fe (H2O) n] ads + Cl− → [Fe (H2O) n Cl]− ads
(3) [Fe (H2O) n Cl]− ads → [Fe (H2O) n Cl] ads + e−
(4) [Fe (H2O) n Cl] ads → Fe2++Cl− + n H+ + n OH−

The hydrogen ions receive electrons, which are transferred from the hydrated steel surface adsorbed with chloride ions, in the evolution of hydrogen at the cathode. The reduction reaction is inferred from the equation (5) [11,83].
(5) [Fe (H2O) n] ads + H+ + e− → 1/2 H2 + Fe + n H++ n OH−

The electrochemical process at the anode and cathode is prominently interfered with when [EHSBT]I or [EHSBT]Br is supplied to the solution. It can be found that these two inhibitors are presented as ionic and molecular states in the solution owing to their own temperature-dependent dissociation [5]. Iodide ions and bromide ions with greater size than chloride ions are preferentially adsorbed on the electrified surface, whereupon the electrostatic attraction facilitates the cation approaching to the surface and releasing electrons for the generation ofa coordination bond through these activated sites containing S, O, C = C, two benzene rings [86]. However, the anti-bonding orbitals of N and benzene rings were subsequently occupied with the excess electrons from the surface iron atoms, creating complexes via back donating in virtue of the electrostatic repulsion. Consequently, the corrosion is successfully hindered by a compressed film including these complexes [9,75]. In addition, [EHSBT]+ is directly adsorbed on the cathode areas of the substrate, retaining the reduction of hydrogen. As the size of positive adsorbate is relatively large, huge amounts of the water molecules attached to the surface can be evacuated and replaced by [EHSBT]+. The compressed film composed of cations has stronger hydrophobicity and isolates the corrosive medium [42,43,87–92]. Moreover, the aggressive medium is isolated by a dense membrane with stronger hydrophobicity composed of cations. According to equations (6–12), the corrosion is suppressed by the inhibitors appearing in the molecular and ionic states (chlorine and bromine replaced by Χ) [8,29,50,67,72].
(6) [EHSBT] Χ → [EHSBT]++ Χ −
(7) [Fe (H2O) n] ads + Χ − → [Fe (H2O) n Χ −] ads
(8) [Fe (H2O) n Cl−] ads + Χ − → [Fe (H2O) n Χ −] ads + Cl−
(9) [Fe (H2O) n Χ −] ads + [EHSBT]+ → [Fe (H2O) n Χ −[EHSBT]+] ads
(10) [Fe (H2O) n Cl−] ads + [EHSBT]+ → [Fe (H2O) n [EHSBT]+] ads + Cl−
(11) [Fe (H2O) n Cl−] ads + [EHSBT]Χ → [Fe (H2O) n [EHSBT]Χ] ads + Cl−

Cathodic reaction:
(12) [Fe (H2O) n] ads + [EHSBT]+ + e− → [Fe [EHSBT]] ads

Whenthetemperatureofcorrosionsystemrises, the thermal motion of hydrogen chloride molecules has been accelerated and the chloride ion permeability is improved. At the same time, the weakening physical adsorption promotes the desorption of iodide ions adsorbed upon the hydrated surface. However, as the temperature rises, the adsorption of cation on the surface tends to be stable on account of the bonding between the cation and surface iron atoms. Therefore, the detachment of iodine ions under elevated temperatures is represented in the equation (13). After that, iodine ions with stronger reducibility in preference to iron atoms are oxidized to convert into iodine, which further react with the iodine ions in the environment and dissolve in the water phase, as shown in the following equations (14), (15) [93]. Although the decrease of iodine ions attenuates the interaction between cations and surface atoms, the active areas of chemical adsorption are dominantly distributed in the cation structure, which still reveals the superior corrosion inhibition nature of [EHSBT]I.
(13) [Fe (H2O) n I− [EHSBT]+] ads → I− + [Fe (H2O) n [EHSBT]+] ads
(14) I−+e− → I2
(15) I2 + I−→ I3 −

On the contrary, [EHSBT]Br is combined with the substrate iron to fabricate an even protective film through the synergistic effect of coordination and back donation [5], which is more stable and difficult to be eroded by a high temperature acid.

ce:list-item.ce:para: [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br act as eco-friendly and excellent anti-acid corrosion inhibitors for mild steel at different temperatures (303–333 K). [EHSBT]I shows better corrosion mitigation with an efficiency of 98.16 % than 89.68 % for [EHSBT]Br in concentration of 0.2 mM at 303 K, while the efficiency of [EHSBT]Br is 97.47 % higher than that of [EHSBT]I (95.43 %) at 333 K.

PDP and EIS investigation indicates that the adsorption of inhibitors [EHSBT]I & [EHSBT]Br on the surface hinders the anode and cathode process and promotes the generation of an stable complex membrane.

XPS, UV–vis and FTIR spectra advocate that the inhibitors [EHSBT]I & [EHSBT]Br chemisorb on the substrate to prepare complex barriers, while a protective layer is determined by SEM-EDS. The DFT calculation containing Mulliken charge distribution and Fukui index analysis reveals that the active sites of the [EHSBT]I and [EHSBT]Br are throughout the complete molecule, and [EHSBT]I displays the more powerful reactivity.

A flat adsorption configuration and chemisorption is determined by MD along with RDF, and higher interaction energy of [EHSBT]I represents stronger adsorption capability, corresponding to superior corrosion inhibition performance. The metallic safety protection is hence successfully established based upon the combination of theory and experiments.
